one_line_takeaway: A real-world three-company study of 71,309 phishing emails shows QR-code 'quishing' is statistically as effective as traditional button phishing (and harder to filter), that cheap OSINT+free-LLM emails can hook 30%+ of readers at localized firms, and that self-reported awareness tracks measured susceptibility.
key_finding: Quishing is just as effective as traditional button phishing at getting users to the landing page: aggregate E_B click-through 8.01% (950/11859 read) vs E_Q 8.49% (1970/25172 read), chi-square=0.353, one-tailed p=.276 (no significant difference), and a TOST test confirms equivalence within +/-1% (lower-bound p=.000042, upper-bound p=.0034). This is notable because quishing evades URL filters yet was expected to underperform due to the extra scanning effort. Separately, cheap OSINT+free-LLM emails can be very effective, especially at smaller/localized firms: at C_m 31.5% of readers visited the page and 14.9% submitted credentials, and at tiny C_s 66.6% visited; effectiveness dropped sharply at the multinational C_h (E_L only 4.5% visited vs 8.1%/7.9% for E_B/E_Q), yet still harvested 243 credentials there. Finally, perceived awareness strongly predicted susceptibility across the three firms (PPA-scores 3.108/4.068/4.393 for C_s/C_m/C_h vs PS-scores 38.24/15.01/7.14; linear regression p<.001, Spearman rho=-1.0).
core_contribution: A multi-organization field study measuring how effective two emerging phishing-email vectors are against real employees: (i) QR-code 'quishing' emails versus visually-identical traditional click-through-button emails, and (ii) phishing emails written by feeding OSINT into a free LLM (ChatGPT 3.5 Turbo). The authors ran three live phishing simulations across three real Central-European companies of differing size (small hospitality SME C_s <250 employees; medium finance firm C_m ~1500; huge multinational manufacturer C_h >30000), sending 71,309 emails in total, and complemented this with a 131-person employee survey on 'perceived phishing awareness' (PPA). The headline empirical claims are the authors' own (from their own simulations and Table 2), not borrowed from cited work: quishing is statistically indistinguishable from button phishing at luring users, OSINT+LLM emails can be highly effective and cheap, and self-reported awareness correlates strongly with measured susceptibility across the three firms.
threat_model: An external attacker aiming to steal employee credentials (userid/password) of a specific target company via targeted phishing emails. The attacker is assumed to know some employees' names and email addresses (trivially inferable from each other) and the company's email provider (inferable via MX lookups), and sets up a credential-harvesting page mimicking the org's branding whose URL is not yet blocklisted (e.g. via cloned pages on fresh URLs). To conceal the malicious URL the attacker either embeds it in a click-through button or encodes it in a QR code; the quishing variant is emphasized because QR codes bypass URL-based email filters and, being scanned on a personal smartphone off the corporate network, evade org-wide controls (firewalls, VPN, private DNS). A second attacker capability is combining freely-available OSINT (employer-rating site, LinkedIn posts, press releases) with a free public LLM to cheaply craft a persuasive, company-tailored pretext (a fake internal survey invitation). The study operationalizes 'effectiveness' as landing-page-visits / emails-read, deliberately excluding unopened emails and post-landing behavior as outside the email content's influence.
limitations: Only three companies participated, all in Central Europe, so the PPA-vs-susceptibility regression is fit on just three data points -- the reported R^2=1.0 and rho=-1.0 are near-tautological with n=3 and should be read as suggestive, not conclusive (the authors themselves frame it cautiously). C_s's sample is tiny (9-13 emails read per condition), making its per-company results statistically weak (chi-square gave p=1.0). E_L differs from E_B/E_Q on many axes (survey pretext with no loss cue vs account-lockout urgency), so the paper deliberately does not statistically compare E_L against the others, limiting causal claims about LLM effectiveness. For C_h the E_Q simulation was run by the company itself, targeted more employees, and logged no credential-submission data (hence 'n/a' cells and asterisked aggregates). NDA constraints hide company identities, exact targeting percentages, and the LLM jailbreak prompts, reducing reproducibility. Company-specific confounds (language German vs English, presence/absence of external-email banners, differing simulation frameworks) are acknowledged but not fully controlled. Survey respondents self-selected and cannot be matched to specific simulation outcomes, so the PPA-susceptibility link is company-level, not individual-level.

datasets_used: Three real Central-European companies: C_s (hospitality SME, 50-250 employees, GoPhish), C_m (finance, ~1500 employees, MS Defender), C_h (manufacturing multinational, >30000 employees, MS Defender)
71,309 phishing emails sent in total (18,339 E_B button + 34,610 E_Q QR-code + 18,360 E_L OSINT/LLM)
131 employee survey responses on perceived phishing awareness (13 C_s, 82 C_m, 36 C_h)
OSINT sources: Kununu employer-rating site, LinkedIn company posts, company press releases (gathered Feb 2024)
LLM used to generate E_L: ChatGPT 3.5 Turbo (free version, Q2 2024)
quantitative_results: RQ1 aggregate: E_B 8.01% page-visited/read (950/11859); E_Q 8.49% (1970/25172); chi-square=0.353, one-tailed p=.276 (no significant difference); effect size 0.0031
RQ1 TOST equivalence at +/-1%: lower-bound p=.000042, upper-bound p=.0034 (both <.05 => practically equivalent); click-rate difference 0.18%, 95% CI (-0.41%, 0.78%)
RQ1 per-company chi-square: C_s chi=0.0 p=1.0; C_m chi=0.514 p=1.0; C_h chi=0.709 p=.2 (none reject null)
RQ2 C_m E_L: 31.5% of readers visited landing page (125/397), 14.9% submitted credentials (59/397)
RQ2 C_s E_L: 66.6% visited page (8/12), 25.0% submitted credentials (3/12) -- but tiny sample
RQ2 C_h E_L: 4.5% visited (499/11025), 243 credentials harvested; lower than C_h E_B (8.1%) and E_Q (7.9%)
Credentials submitted / email read aggregate: E_B 4.6% (541), E_L 2.7% (305); E_Q credential data unavailable for C_h
RQ3 PPA-scores: C_s 3.108, C_m 4.068, C_h 4.393; PS-scores: C_s 38.24, C_m 15.01, C_h 7.14
RQ3 linear regression: R^2=1.0, slope=-24.2, intercept=113.45, p<.001; Spearman rho=-1.0, p=0 (n=3 companies)
C_s incident signal: 11 employees phoned/messaged IT questioning the emails' legitimacy
baselines_compared: Internal control: E_B (traditional click-through-button phishing) serves as the baseline against which E_Q (quishing) and E_L (OSINT+LLM) are measured within each company

External prior-work benchmarks cited for context (NOT the authors' own runs): Sharevski et al. quishing MTurk study where only 14.5% refused to scan; IBM X-Force OSINT+ChatGPT test on 800 healthcare employees; Bethany et al. ChatGPT phishing on 9000 university members; Heiding et al. spear-phishing on 101 volunteers -- the paper notes its own OSINT+LLM rates exceed some prior benchmarks
